paper_title: Ensemble Kalman Fitting and Experimental Design in Climate Data Assimilation

methods.description: The Ensemble Kalman Fitting (EKF) method is used to assimilate climate data by minimizing errors between model simulations and observations. It updates the ensemble mean and anomalies without requiring a cycle, suitable for paleo-climatic settings.

steps.description: Mathematically, data assimilation can be expressed as a minimization of errors. The following cost function is minimized.
steps.searchable_summary: minimize cost function for data assimilation

steps.description: Assuming normally distributed probabilities, this cost function can be minimized with a Kalman filter, where the best estimate of the true atmospheric state x is the analysis x_a.
steps.searchable_summary: apply Kalman filter to minimize cost function

steps.description: We work with a sequential implementation of the Ensemble Kalman Filter. This variation is computationally much less demanding and allows updating the ensemble members individually without explicitly updating the covariance matrices.
To account for a bias occurring in the analysis covariance, we apply the ensemble square root filter.
steps.searchable_summary: apply ensemble square root filter to correct bias

steps.description: The assimilation procedure can be split into an update of the ensemble mean and an update of the anomalies about the ensemble mean.

In numerical weather prediction, the procedure is cycled, but in a paleo-climatic setting, a cycle is not required. This allows us to first run the entire simulation and assimilate the data afterward (off-line assimilation).
steps.searchable_summary: perform off-line assimilation for paleo-climatic data

methods.method_name: Experimental Design and Proxy Forward Model
methods.description: This method addresses systematic biases in climate models by assimilating anomalies over 70-year periods, ensuring that variability on longer scales stems from the model while shorter scales are influenced by observations.

steps.description: We solve the problem of systematic biases by assimilating anomalies for 70-year periods around the current year.
To assess whether the model produces realistic multi-decadal to centennial variability, we compared CCC400 global mean land temperature with instrumental global mean temperatures from the CRUTEM4.
steps.searchable_summary: compare model temperature with CRUTEM4 data

steps.description: We produced a simple reconstruction of global mean land temperature from fitting 400-year time series of external forcings to CRUTEM4 in a multiple regression approach with an autoregressive term.
